elements.sent-2411-001: This paper presents an alternative formulation of the geodesic normal distribution on the sphere, building on the work of \cite{Hauberg2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-002: While the isotropic version of this distribution is naturally defined on the sphere, the anisotropic version requires projecting points from the hypersphere onto the tangent space.
elements.sent-2411-003: In contrast, our approach removes the dependence on the tangent space and defines the geodesic normal distribution directly on the sphere.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, we demonstrate that the density contours of this distribution are exactly ellipses on the sphere, providing intriguing alternative characterizations for describing this locus of points.
elements.sent-2411-005: Directional data refers to observations whose support lies on the unit hypersphere $\mathbb{S ^p:= \{\bx \in \mathbb{R ^{p+1}\colon \|\bx\|^2=\bx^\top \bx = 1\}$ in $\mathbb{R ^{p+1}$, $p \ge 1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Isotropic normal distributions are commonly used to model such data, with the resulting density contours forming (hyper)spherical circles on $\mathbb{S ^p$.}
elements.sent-2411-007: In this setting, two main geometric frameworks are used to define distances.
elements.sent-2411-008: The extrinsic framework employs the Euclidean norm, where distances between objects are computed after embedding them into Euclidean space \citep{bhattacharya2012nonparametric .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The classical von Mises-Fisher distribution arises from using the Euclidean distance on $\mathbb{S ^p$.}
elements.sent-2411-010: In contrast, the intrinsic framework defines distances via the shortest-path geodesics (great circles) on the hypersphere, leading to the (isotropic) geodesic normal distribution on $\mathbb{S ^p$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: This term was first introduced by \cite{coeurjolly2012geodesic for $p=1$, and later generalized to higher dimensions by \citet{Hauberg2018 , who referred to it as the spherical normal.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: A comprehensive study of this distribution was provided by \cite{you2022parameter .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Extensions to anisotropic distributions have been proposed within both frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, \citet{Kent1982 introduced a 5-parameter distribution on the sphere $\mathbb{S ^2$ that uses the Euclidean norm, but does not account for the curvature of the sphere.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Other Kent-like alternatives that offer the same advantages as the ESAG model include the scaled von Mises-Fisher family suggested by \cite{scealy2019scaled , which incorporates an additional parameter to control tail-weight.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Within the intrinsic framework, \cite{Hauberg2018 introduced the anisotropic spherical normal distribution on $\mathbb{S ^p$, which takes into account the curvature of the sphere.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: This distribution employs the logarithm map to project points from the hypersphere onto its tangent space, building upon the general Riemannian normal distribution described in \citet{pennec2006intrinsic .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The main aim of this paper is to present an alternative formulation of Hauberg spherical normal distribution that avoids its reliance on the tangent space.
elements.sent-2411-019: In this new formulation, the distribution is expressed directly on the spherical manifold, providing a more natural representation.
elements.sent-2411-020: Furthermore, it is shown that the density contours of this distribution are true ellipses on the sphere, in contrast to other anisotropic distributions, thereby offering a clearer geometric understanding of the distribution.
elements.sent-2411-021: The rest of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-022: Section \ref{sec:normal provides a brief overview of isotropic and anisotropic normal distributions on the sphere.}
elements.sent-2411-023: In Section \ref{sec:gn , we present our alternative formulation of the anisotropic geodesic normal distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Finally, Section \ref{sec:sph_ell exhibits several equivalent equations for defining a spherical ellipse and demonstrates that the density contours of the anisotropic geodesic normal distribution correspond to spherical ellipses.}
elements.sent-2411-025: For a given distance $d$ on the $p$-dimensional sphere $\mathbb S^p$, the class of isotropic normal distributions with respect to $d$ consists of those whose density is proportional to $\exp\{-\frac{\kappa 2d(\bx,\bmu)^2\}$ with respect to the Lebesgue measure $\sigma_p({\mathsf d}\bx)$ on $\mathbb S^p$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Here, $\bmu\in\mathbb S^p$ represents the mean direction and $\kappa>0$ is a concentration parameter.
elements.sent-2411-027: Their density contours are given by $\{\bx\in\mathbb S^p\colon d(\bx,\bmu)=\rho\}$ for some $\rho>0$.
elements.sent-2411-028: This is precisely the definition, as a locus of points, of a spherical circle with center $\bmu$ and radius $\rho$ (with respect to $d$).
elements.sent-2411-029: When using the Euclidean distance on the hypersphere, given by $d_E(\bx,\bmu)=\{2(1-\bx^\top\bmu)\}^{1/2}$ for $\bx,\bmu\in\mathbb S^p$, we obtain the von Mises-Fisher distribution, whose density is $f_{\rm vMF (\bx;\bmu,\kappa)=C_{\rm vMF (\kappa)^{-1}\exp(\kappa\,\bx^\top\bmu)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Here, $C_{\rm vMF (\kappa)=\int_{\mathbb S^p}\exp(\kappa\,\bx^\top\bmu)\sigma_p(\mathsf d\bx)$ denotes the normalizing constant, whose explicit formula is also well known \citep[see][Section 9.3.2]{Mardia1999a .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In contrast, the geodesic distance is defined as $d_G(\bx,\bmu)=\arccos(\bx^\top\bmu)$, which represents the length of the shortest arc between $\bx$ and $\bmu$ along $\mathbb S^p$.
elements.sent-2411-032: Hence, the (isotropic) geodesic normal distribution has density fuction $f_{\rm GN (\bx;\bmu,\kappa)=C_{\rm GN (\kappa)^{-1}\exp\{-\frac{\kappa 2\arccos^2(\bx^\top\bmu)\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: For the case $p=1$, an explicit formula for the normalizing constant $C_{\rm GN (\kappa)=\int_{\mathbb S^p}\exp\{-\frac\kappa2\arccos^2(\bx^\top\bmu)\}\sigma_p(\mathsf d\bx)$ was derived by \cite{coeurjolly2012geodesic .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: For higher dimensions, formulas were provided in \citet{Hauberg2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Various approaches have been proposed to extend those existing models to obtain anisotropic normal distributions.
elements.sent-2411-036: This distribution is a generalization of the von Mises-Fisher distribution, which corresponds to the particular choices of $\beta=0$ and $\bm\gamma_1=\bmu$.
elements.sent-2411-037: According to \citet{Kent1982 , the contours of $f_{\rm K}$ are ``approximately ellipses'' centered at $\bm\gamma_1$, with major and minor axes $\bm\gamma_2$ and $\bm\gamma_3$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The parameter $\kappa$ controls the concentration, while $\beta$ determines the ``ovalness" of the density contours.
elements.sent-2411-039: In the intrinsic setting, \cite{Hauberg2018 introduced an anisotropic spherical normal distribution on $\mathbb S^p$.}
elements.sent-2411-040: This approach is based on the fact that any point $\bx\in\mathbb S^p\setminus\{-\bmu\}$ can be mapped to the tangent space $\mathcal T_\bmu=\{\bx\in\mathbb R^{p+1}\colon\bx^\top\bmu=0\}$ via the so-called logarithm map, which is defined as ${\rm Log _\bmu(\bx)=\{\bx-(\bx^\top\bmu)\bmu\}d_G(\bx,\bmu)/\sin\{d_G(\bx,\bmu)\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: This map is an isometry, so that $d_G(\bx,\bmu)^2={\rm Log _\bmu(\bx)^\top{\rm Log _\bmu(\bx)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The proposal by \citet{Hauberg2018 replaces the latter scalar product with a Mahalanobis distance in the tangent space, resulting in a distribution with density $f_{\rm H (\bx;\bmu,{\bf\Lambda )=C_{\rm H ({\bf\Lambda )^{-1}\exp\{-\frac12{\Log _\bmu(\bx)^\top{\bf\Lambda {\rm Log _\bmu(\bx)\},$ where $\bf\Lambda$ is a symmetric, positive semi-definite $(p+1)\times (p+1)$ matrix, having $\bmu$ as an eigenvector with associated null eigenvalue.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: While an exact expression for the normalizing constant $C_{\rm H (\bf\Lambda)$ is not available, an accurate approximation was derived in \citet{Hauberg2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: In the following, we provide an alternative representation for \citeauthor{Hauberg2018 's anisotropic geodesic normal distribution that does not rely on the tangent space for its formulation.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We focus on $p=2$, which will be the primary case discussed in Section \ref{sec:sph_ell .}
elements.sent-2411-046: However, most of the arguments can be easily extended to an arbitrary dimension $p$ with only minor adjustments.
elements.section-2411-003: An alternative formulation of the anisotropic geodesic normal distribution
elements.sent-2411-047: As noted above, the matrix $\bf\Lambda$ is positive semi-definite, with a null eigenvalue corresponding to the eigenvector $\bmu$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Let us denote by $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ the remaining nonnegative eigenvalues of $\bf\Lambda$, and choose associated eigenvectors $\bm\eta$ and $\bm\xi$ so that ${\bf \Gamma =(\bmu|\bm\eta|\bm\xi)$ is an orthogonal matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Thus, $\{\bmu,\bm\eta,\bm\xi\}$ is an orthonormal basis, and any $\bx\in\mathbb R^3$ can be expressed as $\bx=(\bx^\top\bmu)\bmu+(\bx^\top\bm\eta)\bm\eta+(\bx^\top\bm\xi)\bm\xi$, leading to $\bx^\top{\bf\Lambda}\bx=\lambda_1(\bx^\top\bm\eta)^2+\lambda_2(\bx^\top\bm\xi)^2$.
elements.sent-2411-050: On the other hand, for $\bx\in\mathbb S^2$ we have $d_G(\bx,\bmu)=\arccos(\bx^\top\bmu)$, resulting in $\sin^2\{d_G(\bx,\bmu)\}=1-(\bx^\top\bmu)^2=(\bx^\top\bm\eta)^2+(\bx^\top\bm\xi)^2$.. Therefore, \begin{equation}\label{eq:quadratic} {\rm Log}_\bmu(\bx)^\top{\bm\Lambda}{\rm Log}_\bmu(\bx)=\arccos^2(\bx^\top\bmu)\frac{\lambda_1(\bx^\top\bm\eta)^2+\lambda_2(\bx^\top\bm\xi)^2}{(\bx^\top\bm\eta)^2+(\bx^\top\bm\xi)^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-051: Consequently, Hauberg anisotropic geodesic normal distribution on the sphere can be alternatively defined in terms of the density \begin{equation}\label{eq:fgn} f_{\rm GN}(\bx;\lambda_1,\lambda_2,{\bf \Gamma})=C_{\rm GN}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)^{-1}\exp\Big\{-\frac12\arccos^2(\bx^\top\bmu)\frac{\lambda_1(\bx^\top\bm\eta)^2+\lambda_2(\bx^\top\bm\xi)^2}{(\bx^\top\bm\eta)^2+(\bx^\top\bm\xi)^2}\Big\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-052: Several important observations can be made from equation \eqref{eq:fgn .}
elements.sent-2411-053: First, it enables the definition of the geodesic normal distribution on the sphere $\mathbb S^2$ without resorting to its tangent space.
elements.sent-2411-054: Second, expression \eqref{eq:fgn facilitates the comparison with \citeauthor{Kent1982 distribution, since the elements needed to define the distribution are very similar, namely an orthogonal matrix $\bf\Gamma=(\bmu|\bm\eta|\bm\xi)$ and two nonnegative parameters $\lambda_1,\lambda_2$, which together account for a total of five free parameters.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The vector $\bmu$ represents the mean direction, and as we will show in Section \ref{sec:sph_ell , the distribution $f_{\rm GN}$ has true elliptical contours on the sphere (not just approximate ones), with $\bm \eta$ and $\bm\xi$ serving as the axes, and $\lambda_1,\lambda_2$ being inversely related to their lengths.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In this regard, the parameters of this geodesic normal distribution have a more intuitive interpretation than those of Kent distribution.
elements.sent-2411-057: Finally, note that setting $\lambda_1=\lambda_2=\kappa$ results in the isotropic geodesic normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-058: The terms ``oval'' or ``elliptical'' are often used to describe the contours of many distributions on $\mathbb{S ^2$, although usually without a precise meaning.}
elements.sent-2411-059: However, the notion of spherical ellipse has an accurate definition within the field of Spherical Geometry, and it is a topic that has been extensively studied over the years.
elements.sent-2411-060: A recent overview is provided by \citet[][Chapter 10.1]{glaeser2016universe .}
elements.sent-2411-061: In this section, we derive several equivalent equations for defining a spherical ellipse, which may be of mathematical interest.
elements.sent-2411-062: Furthermore, we demonstrate that the density contours of the anisotropic geodesic normal distribution indeed form true spherical ellipses.
elements.sent-2411-063: A spherical ellipse is defined as the locus of points on the sphere for which the sum of the geodesic distances to two fixed (non-antipodal) points remains constant.
elements.sent-2411-064: Specifically, given two points $\bbf_1,\bbf_2\in\mathbb S^2$ with geodesic distance $d_G(\bbf_1,\bbf_2)=\arccos({\bbf_1}\!\!^\top\bbf_2)=2\gamma<\pi$, and an angle $\alpha$, the spherical ellipse with foci $\bbf_1,\bbf_2$ and major axis length $2\alpha$ is defined as the set of points $\bx\in\mathbb S^2$ satisfying \begin{equation}\label{eq:se1} d_G(\bx,\bbf_1)+d_G(\bx,\bbf_2)=2\alpha.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: The great circle on the sphere passing through the two foci $\bbf_1$ and $\bbf_2$ determines the major axis of the ellipse.
elements.sent-2411-066: The center of the ellipse is the midpoint of the shortest arc joining $\bbf_1$ and $\bbf_2$, that is, $\bmu=(\bbf_1+\bbf_2)/\|\bbf_1+\bbf_2\|=(\bbf_1+\bbf_2)/(2\cos\gamma)\in\mathbb S^2$.
elements.sent-2411-067: Thus, the angle $\gamma$ represents the geodesic distance from either focus to the ellipse center, and must satisfy $\gamma<\alpha<\pi-\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-068: Figure~\ref{fig:sph_ell shows a spherical ellipse with foci given by $\bbf_1=(\cos\gamma,\sin\gamma,0)$ and $\bbf_2=(\cos\gamma,-\sin\gamma,0)$ for $\gamma=\pi/6$, center $\bmu=(1,0,0)$ and $\alpha=\pi/4$.}

\addFGBGplot[domain=0:2*pi, samples=100, samples y=1] (0, {sin(deg(x))}, {cos(deg(x))}); \addFGBGplot[domain=0:2*pi, samples=100, samples y=1] ({sin(deg(x))}, 0, {cos(deg(x))}); \end{axis} \end{tikzpicture} \caption{Spherical ellipse with foci $\bbf_1=(\cos\gamma,\sin\gamma,0)$ and $\bbf_2=(\cos\gamma,-\sin\gamma,0)$ for $\gamma=\pi/6$, center $\bmu=(1,0,0)$ and $\alpha=\pi/4$.} \label{fig:sph_ell}
elements.sent-2411-069: Although \eqref{eq:se1 provides the most straightforward definition of a spherical ellipse, alternative forms are often more convenient.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Instead of directly using the foci, the ellipse can be characterized by its center $\bmu$ and the direction $\bm\eta$ defining the major axis.
elements.sent-2411-071: To determine $\bm\eta$, we need to find a unit vector from the space spanned by $\bbf_1$ and $\bbf_2$ that is orthogonal to $\bmu$.
elements.sent-2411-072: It can be checked that the vector $\bm\eta=(\bbf_1-\bbf_2)/(2\sin\gamma)$ satisfies the required conditions.
elements.sent-2411-073: Next, express the foci $\bbf_1$ and $\bbf_2$ in terms of $\bmu$ and $\bm\eta$, resulting in $\bbf_1=\bm\mu\cos\gamma+\bm\eta\sin \gamma$ and $\bbf_2=\bm\mu\cos\gamma-\bm\eta\sin \gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Substituting these into the original ellipse \eqref{eq:se1 , we obtain \begin{equation}\label{eq:se2} \arccos\big(\bx^\top\bm\mu\cos\gamma+\bx^\top\bm\eta\sin\gamma\big)+\arccos\big(\bx^\top\bm\mu\cos\gamma-\bx^\top\bm\eta\sin\gamma\big)=2\alpha,\end{equation} with the constraints $0\leq\gamma<\pi/2$ and $\gamma<\alpha<\pi-\gamma$, where $\bm\eta\in\mathbb S^2$ is orthogonal to $\bmu$.}
elements.sent-2411-075: In the case where $\gamma=0$, this equation simplifies to a spherical circle with center $\bmu$ and radius $\alpha<\pi$, as it reduces to $\arccos(\bx^\top\bm\mu)=\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Alternatively, the ellipse equation can be written in terms of the semi-minor axis length $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-077: Using the spherical Pythagorean theorem for the minor axis vertex it follows that $\cos \alpha = \cos \beta \cos \gamma$, so we find that $\cos\gamma=(\cos\alpha)/(\cos\beta)$ and $\sin\gamma=\{1-(\cos^2\alpha)/(\cos^2\beta)\}^{1/2}$.
elements.sent-2411-078: Substituting these expressions into equation (\ref{eq:se2 ) gives \begin{equation}\label{eq:se3} \arccos\big\{\bx^\top\bm\mu\tfrac{\cos\alpha}{\cos\beta}+\bx^\top\bm\eta(1-\tfrac{\cos^2\alpha}{\cos^2\beta})^{1/2}\big\}+\arccos\big\{\bx^\top\bm\mu\tfrac{\cos\alpha}{\cos\beta}-\bx^\top\bm\eta(1-\tfrac{\cos^2\alpha}{\cos^2\beta})^{1/2}\big\}=2\alpha,\end{equation} with the constraints $0<\beta\leq\alpha<\pi$, and $\bm\eta\in\mathbb S^2$ orthogonal to $\bmu\in\mathbb S^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Note that this equation involves five free parameters, similar to the general equation of an ellipse in $\mathbb R^2$.
elements.sent-2411-080: In this case, the equation of a spherical circle with radius $\rho$ is obtained by setting $\alpha=\beta=\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Figure~\ref{fig:sph_ell2 shows three examples of spherical ellipses centered at $\bmu=(1,0,0)$, each with different parameter configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The first one (left panel), with $\alpha=\beta=\pi/12$, corresponds to a spherical circle.
elements.sent-2411-083: The second one (center panel), with $\bm\eta=(0,1,0)$ and $\alpha=\pi/6$, $\beta=\pi/12$, shows an ellipse aligned with the equator and the reference meridian.
elements.sent-2411-084: The third one (right panel) has the same axis lengths but a different orientation for the major axis, with $\bm\eta=(0,\frac{\sqrt2 2,\frac{\sqrt2 2)$.}}

elements.sent-2411-085: Some references mention even simpler equations in case the ellipse is assumed to be in ``standard position''.
elements.sent-2411-086: In $\mathbb R^2$, this refers to an ellipse centered at the origin and aligned with the coordinate axes.
elements.sent-2411-087: In the spherical case, the standard ellipse can be considered to be centered at $\bmu=(1,0,0)$, with the major axis along the direction of $\bm\eta=(0,1,0)$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Then, writing $\bx=(x_1,x_2,x_3)\in\mathbb R^3$, equation \eqref{eq:se2 simplifies to $\arccos(x_1\cos\gamma+x_2\sin\gamma)+\arccos(x_1\cos\gamma-x_2\sin\gamma)=2\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Next, after some straightforward calculations we get \begin{equation}\label{eq:se4} x_1^2\frac{\cos^2\gamma}{\cos^2\alpha}+x_2^2\frac{\sin^2\gamma}{\sin^2\alpha}=1,\end{equation} as shown in \citet[][pp. 446--448]{glaeser2016universe .}
elements.sent-2411-090: This recalls the usual equation $x_1^2/a^2+x_2^2/b^2=1$ of a standard ellipse in $\mathbb R^2$.
elements.sent-2411-091: However, in contrast to the Euclidean case, the transition from equation \eqref{eq:se3 to \eqref{eq:se4 introduces a spurious curve.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: While the set of points satisfying equation \eqref{eq:se3 corresponds to a single curve on the sphere (as shown previously in Figure~\ref{fig:sph_ell ), the locus of points satisfying equation \eqref{eq:se4 consists of two antipodal ellipses, centered at $\bmu$ and $-\bmu$, respectively, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:two_ell .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: This outcome is not so surprising, since equation \eqref{eq:se4 corresponds to a elliptic cylinder in $\mathbb R^3$, and its intersection with $\mathbb S^2$ gives rise to the two spherical ellipses.}

elements.sent-2411-094: To address the previous issue, we derive an alternative equation that includes only one spherical ellipse.
elements.sent-2411-095: Our development is based on the tangent space $\mathcal T_\bmu$, but no mapping to $\mathcal T_\bmu$ is eventually required for the final equation.
elements.sent-2411-096: As discussed in Section \ref{sec:normal , let $\bm\eta,\bm\xi\in\mathbb S^2$ be vectors such that $\{\bmu,\bm\eta,\bm\xi\}$ form an orthonormal basis of $\mathbb R^3$ and consider the orthogonal matrix ${\bf\Gamma =(\bmu|\bm\eta|\bm\xi)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Let $\mathcal E\subset \mathcal S^2$ represent the locus of points $\bx\in\mathbb S^2$ satisfying \begin{equation}\label{eq:se5} \arccos^2(\bx^\top\bmu)\frac{(\bx^\top\bm\eta)^2/\alpha^2+(\bx^\top\bm\xi)^2/\beta^2}{(\bx^\top\bm\eta)^2+(\bx^\top\bm\xi)^2}=1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-098: In view of \eqref{eq:quadratic , the image of $\mathcal E$ through $\Log_\bmu$ forms an ellipse $\mathcal E^*$ in the tangent space $\mathcal T_\bmu$, since it consists of all the points $\by=\Log_\bmu(\bx)\in\mathcal T_\bmu$ satisfying $\by^\top{\bf\Lambda}\by=1$, where ${\bf\Lambda ={\bf \Gamma {\bf D {\bf\Gamma ^\top$ and $\mathbf D$ is the diagonal matrix ${\rm diag (0,1/\alpha^2,1/\beta^2)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: That is, in terms of the basis $\{\bm\eta,\bm\xi\}$ of the vector space $\mathcal T_\bmu$, the set $\mathcal E^*$ is the ellipse with equation $(\by^\top\bm\eta)^2/\alpha^2+(\by^\top\bm\xi)^2/\beta^2=1$, where the major and minor axes are determined by $\bm\eta$ and $\bm\xi$, with lengths $\alpha$ and $\beta$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-100: Then, the foci of the ellipse $\mathcal E^*\subset\mathcal T_\bmu$ are located at $\gamma\bm\eta$ and $-\gamma\bm\eta$, with $0\leq\gamma<\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-101: Therefore, $\mathcal E^*$ can be equivalently described as the locus of points $\by\in\mathcal T_\bmu$ such that $d_\mathcal T(\by,\gamma\bm\eta)+d_\mathcal T(\by,-\gamma\bm\eta)=2\alpha$, where $d_\mathcal T$ denotes the Euclidean distance in $\mathcal T_\bmu$, defined as $d_\mathcal T(\by_1,\by_2)^2=(\by_1-\by_2)^\top(\by_1-\by_2)$ for $\by_1,\by_2\in\mathcal T_\bmu$.
elements.sent-2411-102: Since $\Log_\bmu$ is an isometry, this implies that $\mathcal E$ is the locus of points $\bx\in\mathbb S^2$ such that $d_G(\bx,\bbf_1)+d_G(\by,\bbf_2)=2\alpha$, where $\bbf_1=\Log_\bmu^{-1 (\gamma\bm\eta)$ and $\bbf_2=\Log_\bmu^{-1 (-\gamma\bm\eta)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Hence, $\mathcal E$ is a spherical ellipse.
elements.sent-2411-104: Thus, \eqref{eq:se5 represents the equation of the spherical ellipse, with axes determined by $\bm\eta$ and $\bm\xi$ and semi-axis lengths $\alpha$ and $\beta$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-105: This demonstrates, on the one hand, that the density contours of the geodesic normal distribution are spherical ellipses.
elements.sent-2411-106: On the other hand, equation \eqref{eq:se5 offers an alternative equation for a spherical ellipse, which appears to be previously unknown.}
